# Home

## Our Mission

*We educate leaders for Science, Medicine and Business!*

We are Loschmidt Laboratories based at the [Masaryk University](https://www.muni.cz/en) and the [International Clinical Research Center](https://fnusa-icrc.org/en) of St. Anne’s University Hospital Brno. Our laboratories are located at the University Campus Bohunice in Brno, Czech Republic.

Proteins are natural building blocks of all living organisms and participate in virtually every process within cells. Many natural proteins are enzymes that catalyse biochemical reactions and are essential for metabolism. Proteins can be found everywhere — they play a role in the development of medicines, in food and beverages processing, animal nutrition, textiles, household cleaning, fuels, and energy generation. And therefore the study of proteins is valuable for the whole society.

We conduct interdisciplinary research in the field of protein engineering. We wish to understand the structure-function relationships of proteins and improve their functionalities for biotechnologies. We study the mechanisms of Alzheimer’s disease and develop novel drugs for acute stroke. Our goal is to be recognized as one of the leading protein engineering groups in Europe. We will consistently strive to publish our findings in reputable scientific journals, develop software tools and lab-on-chips, and transfer results to practice.

The key components of our daily activities include collaboration between experimentalists and theoreticians, solidarity among the laboratory team, and mentoring of young colleagues — all while maintaining a friendly and creative working environment to honor the name of [Jan Josef Loschmidt](https://loschmidt.chemi.muni.cz/biography/).

## Profile Publications

## Structure Prediction and Computational Protein Design for Efficient Biocatalysts and Bioactive Proteins

Buller, R., Damborsky, J., Hilvert, D., Bornscheuer, U., 2025, *Angewandte Chemie International Edition* 64: e202421686.

![](https://loschmidt.chemi.muni.cz/peg/wp-content/uploads/2025/06/Buller_Structure_Prediction_Computational_Protein_Design_Angewandte_Chemie_2025-300x201.png)The 2024 Nobel Prize in Chemistry was awarded to David Baker for computational protein design and to Demis Hassabis and John Jumper for developing AlphaFold, a machine-learning-based protein structure prediction tool. These advancements have revolutionised protein research and engineering, enabling applications in medicine, diagnostics, sustainable chemistry, and drug discovery (e.g., modelling therapeutic targets, designing peptide binders, and identifying small molecule ligands). The text further discusses the impact on organic synthesis and future directions in protein engineering, medicinal chemistry, and material design.

![](https://loschmidt.chemi.muni.cz/peg/wp-content/uploads/2025/06/Buller_Structure_Prediction_Computational_Protein_Design_Angewandte_Chemie_2025-300x201.png)

[Read more](https://loschmidt.chemi.muni.cz/peg/publications/structure-prediction-and-computational-protein-design-for-efficient-biocatalysts-and-bioactive-proteins/)

## Engineering Dehalogenase Enzymes Using Variational Autoencoder-Generated Latent Spaces and Microfluidics

Kohout, P., Vasina, M., Majerova, M., Novakova, V., Damborsky, J., Bednar, D., Marek, M., Prokop, Z., Mazurenko, S., 2025, *JACS Au* 5: 838-850.

![](https://loschmidt.chemi.muni.cz/peg/wp-content/uploads/2025/03/Kohout_Engineering_Dehalogenases_JACS_2025_abstract-300x158.jpeg)Here, we present a new machine-learning method based on variational autoencoders and an evolutionary sampling strategy to engineer model enzymes, specifically haloalkane dehalogenases. We generated variants with novel sequences, and three design-build-test cycles improved their solubility from 11% to 75%. Thorough experimental validation, including the use of the microfluidic device Micro-PEX, resulted in 20 multiple-point variants. The most stable variant demonstrated a 3.5-fold increase in activity compared to the template.

![](https://loschmidt.chemi.muni.cz/peg/wp-content/uploads/2025/03/Kohout_Engineering_Dehalogenases_JACS_2025_abstract-300x158.jpeg)

[Read more](https://loschmidt.chemi.muni.cz/peg/publications/engineering-dehalogenase-enzymes-using-variational-autoencoder-generated-latent-spaces-and-microfluidics/)

## Biochemical and Computational Characterization of Haloalkane Dehalogenase Variants Designed by Generative AI: Accelerating the SN2 Step

Gelfand, N., Orel, V., Cui, W., Damborsky, J., Li, C., Prokop, Z., Xie, W. J., Warshel, A., 2025, *Journal of the American Chemical Society* 147: 2747-2755.

![](https://loschmidt.chemi.muni.cz/peg/wp-content/uploads/2025/01/Gelfand_Biochemical_Computational_Characterization_HLD_JACS_2025_Graphical_Abstract.jpeg)Here, we demonstrated that a generative maximum-entropy model can enhance the catalytic activity of designed protein variants. We gained molecular insights into these improvements through simulations and kinetic measurements, achieving a faster SN2 reaction rate than the wild-type enzyme, despite haloalkane dehalogenase being extensively optimized by natural evolution.

![](https://loschmidt.chemi.muni.cz/peg/wp-content/uploads/2025/01/Gelfand_Biochemical_Computational_Characterization_HLD_JACS_2025_Graphical_Abstract.jpeg)

[Read more](https://loschmidt.chemi.muni.cz/peg/publications/biochemical-and-computational-characterization-of-haloalkane-dehalogenase-variants-designed-by-generative-ai-accelerating-the-sn2-step/)

## Azobenzene-Based Photoswitchable Substrates for Advanced Mechanistic Studies of Model Haloalkane Dehalogenase Enzyme Family

Slanska, M., Stackova, L., Marques, S. M., Stacko, P., Martinek, M., Jilek, L., Toul, M., Damborsky, J., Bednar, D., Klan, P., Prokop, Z., 2024, *ACS Catalysis* 14: 11635-11645.

![](https://loschmidt.chemi.muni.cz/peg/wp-content/uploads/2024/08/ACS_Catalysis_Slanska_Graphical_Abstract_2024-300x274.jpeg)Here, we have provided a systematic approach to engineer photoswitchable substrates for enzymes, exemplified by the design of an azobenzene-based probe for haloalkane dehalogenases. This novel substrate enables detailed mechanistic studies combining pre-steady-state kinetics and time-resolved femtosecond crystallography, providing a foundation for rational enzyme engineering and optimization.

![](https://loschmidt.chemi.muni.cz/peg/wp-content/uploads/2024/08/ACS_Catalysis_Slanska_Graphical_Abstract_2024-300x274.jpeg)

[Read more](https://loschmidt.chemi.muni.cz/peg/publications/azobenzene-based-photoswitchable-substrates-for-advanced-mechanistic-studies-of-model-haloalkane-dehalogenase-enzyme-family/)

## AggreProt: A Web Server for Predicting and Engineering Aggregation Prone Regions in Proteins

Planas-Iglesias, J., Borko, S., Swiatkowski, J., Elias, M., Havlasek, M., Salamon, O., Grakova, E., Kunka, A., Martinovic, T., Damborsky, J., Martinovic, J., Bednar, D., 2024, *Nucleic Acids Research* gkae420: 1-11.

![](https://loschmidt.chemi.muni.cz/peg/wp-content/uploads/2024/07/NAR_2024.jpeg)The AggreProt web server is a user-friendly interface to our recently developed predictor targeting protein aggregation. The predictor consists of an ensemble of deep neural networks that were trained on a collection of amyloidogenic hexapeptides WaltzDB. Amyloid aggregation is characteristic of important neurodegenerative human diseases, such as Alzheimer’s or Parkinson’s. We demonstrated the ability of our predictor to detect aggregation-prone regions in non-amyloid aggregation on the haloalkane dehalogenase and luciferase. AggreProt web server requires minimal input and presents comprehensive results: <https://loschmidt.chemi.muni.cz/aggreprot/>.

![](https://loschmidt.chemi.muni.cz/peg/wp-content/uploads/2024/07/NAR_2024.jpeg)

[Read more](https://loschmidt.chemi.muni.cz/peg/publications/aggreprot-a-web-server-for-predicting-and-engineering-aggregation-prone-regions-in-proteins/)

## CoVAMPnet: Comparative Markov State Analysis for Studying Effects of Drug Candidates on Disordered Biomolecules

Marques, S. M., Kouba, P., Legrand, A., Sedlar, J., Disson, L., Planas-Iglesias, J., Sanusi, Z., Kunka, A., Damborsky, J., Pajdla, T., Prokop, Z., Mazurenko, S., Sivic, J., Bednar, D., 2024, *JACS Au* 4: 2228-2245.

![](https://loschmidt.chemi.muni.cz/peg/wp-content/uploads/2024/06/JACS_TOC_graphics-300x162.png)Studying drug effects on intrinsically disordered biomolecules is complex due to their vast conformational space. Here we describe a new framework, CoVAMPnet, which quantifies changes in conformational distribution and dynamics using molecular dynamics trajectories, Markov state models, and unsupervised machine learning. We applied it to Alzheimer’s therapeutics tramiprosate (TMP) and its metabolite 3-sulfopropanoic acid (SPA) and found that both compounds preserve structured conformations of Aβ42, with SPA being more effective. Our data suggest that TMP/SPA may also target other biomolecules than Aβ peptides.

![](https://loschmidt.chemi.muni.cz/peg/wp-content/uploads/2024/06/JACS_TOC_graphics-300x162.png)

[Read more](https://loschmidt.chemi.muni.cz/peg/publications/covampnet-comparative-markov-state-analysis-for-studying-effects-of-drug-candidates-on-disordered-biomolecules/)

## Illuminating the Mechanism and Allosteric Behavior of NanoLuc Luciferase

Nemergut, M., Pluskal, D., Horackova, J., Sustrova, T., Tulis, J., Barta, T., Baatallah, R., Gagnot, G., Novakova, V., Majerova, M., Sedlackova, K., Marques, S. M., Toul, M., Damborsky, J., Prokop, Z., Bednar, D., Janin, Y. L., Marek, M., 2023, *Nature Communications*14: 7864.

![](https://loschmidt.chemi.muni.cz/peg/wp-content/uploads/2023/12/Nemergut_Illuminating_the_Mechanism_and_Allosteric_Behavior_of_NanoLuc_Nature_Communications_TOC_2023.pdf-300x278.jpg)

![](https://loschmidt.chemi.muni.cz/peg/wp-content/uploads/2023/12/Nemergut_Illuminating_the_Mechanism_and_Allosteric_Behavior_of_NanoLuc_Nature_Communications_TOC_2023.pdf-300x278.jpg)

NanoLuc, a superior β-barrel fold luciferase, was engineered 10 years ago but the nature of its catalysis remains puzzling. In this work we reveal that imidazopyrazinone luciferins bind to an intra-barrel catalytic site but also to an allosteric site shaped on the enzyme surface. Structurally, binding to the allosteric site prevents simultaneous binding to the catalytic site, and *vice versa*, through concerted conformational changes. We demonstrate that restructuration of the allosteric site can boost the luminescent reaction in the remote active site. This information is critical to engineering the next-generation of ultrasensitive bioluminescent reporters.

[Read more](https://loschmidt.chemi.muni.cz/peg/publications/illuminating-the-mechanism-and-allosteric-behavior-of-nanoluc-luciferase/)

## Advancing Enzyme’s Stability and Catalytic Efficiency through Synergy of Force-Field Calculations, Evolutionary Analysis, and Machine Learning

Kunka, A., Marques, S. M., Havlasek, M., Vasina, M., Velatova, N., Cengelova, L., Kovar, D., Damborsky, J., Marek, M., Bednar, D., Prokop, Z., 2023, *ACS Catalysis* 13: 12506-12518.

![](https://loschmidt.chemi.muni.cz/peg/wp-content/uploads/2023/09/Kunka_Advancing-Enzymes-Stability_ACS_Catalysis_TOC.png)

![](https://loschmidt.chemi.muni.cz/peg/wp-content/uploads/2023/09/Kunka_Advancing-Enzymes-Stability_ACS_Catalysis_TOC.png)

Here, we compare different strategies for stabilizing enzymes, focusing on the hyperstable haloalkane dehalogenase DhaA115. Our results show that the automated protocols FireProt and PROSS outperformed rational design efforts in identifying multiple-point stabilizing mutations. The study also shows that manual curation of predicted mutations and machine learning algorithms can further improve stability and catalytic efficiency. The work resulted in three highly thermostable haloalkane dehalogenases with improved catalytic efficiencies.

[Read more](https://loschmidt.chemi.muni.cz/peg/publications/advancing-enzymes-stability-and-catalytic-efficiency-through-synergy-of-force-field-calculations-evolutionary-analysis-and-machine-learning/)

## In-depth Analysis of Biocatalysts by Microfluidics: An Emerging Source of Data for Machine Learning

Vasina, M., Kovar, D., Damborsky, J., Ding, Y., Yang, T., deMello, A., Mazurenko, S., Stavrakis, S., Prokop, Z., 2023, *Biotechnology Advances* 66: 108171.

![](https://loschmidt.chemi.muni.cz/peg/wp-content/uploads/2023/07/Vasina_In-depth_Analysis_of_Biocatalysts_by_Microfluidics_Biotechnol_Advances_2023f-scaled.jpg)

![](https://loschmidt.chemi.muni.cz/peg/wp-content/uploads/2023/07/Vasina_In-depth_Analysis_of_Biocatalysts_by_Microfluidics_Biotechnol_Advances_2023f-scaled.jpg)

In this review, we present the state-of-the-art, recent trends, and perspectives in applying microfluidic tools in the analysis of biocatalysts. We discuss the advantages and disadvantages of available technologies, their reproducibility and robustness, and their readiness for routine laboratory use. We also highlight the unexplored potential of microfluidics to leverage the power of machine learning for biocatalyst development.

[Read more](https://loschmidt.chemi.muni.cz/peg/publications/in-depth-analysis-of-biocatalysts-by-microfluidics-an-emerging-source-of-data-for-machine-learning/)

## Identification, Characterization, and Engineering of Glycosylation in Thrombolytics

Toul, M., Slonkova, V., Mican, J., Urminsky, A., Tomkova, M., Sedlak, E., Bednar, D., Damborsky, J., Hernychova, L., Prokop, Z., 2023, *Biotechnology Advances* 66: 108174.

![](https://loschmidt.chemi.muni.cz/peg/wp-content/uploads/2023/07/Toul_Identification_Characterization_Glycosylation_Thrombolytics_TOC-300x261.png)

![](https://loschmidt.chemi.muni.cz/peg/wp-content/uploads/2023/07/Toul_Identification_Characterization_Glycosylation_Thrombolytics_TOC-300x261.png)

Here we summarize current knowledge on computational and experimental identification of glycosylation sites and glycan identity, together with methods used for their reengineering. Practical examples from previous studies focus on modifying glycosylations in thrombolytics, e.g., alteplase, tenecteplase, reteplase, urokinase, saruplase, and desmoteplase. Collected clinical data on these glycoproteins demonstrate the great potential of the glycosylation engineering strategy for the developing a new generation of truly efficient and life-saving thrombolytic drugs.

[Read more](https://loschmidt.chemi.muni.cz/peg/publications/identification-characterization-and-engineering-of-glycosylation-in-thrombolytics/)

## Catalytic Mechanism for *Renilla*-type Luciferases

Schenkmayerova, A., Toul, M., Pluskal, D., Baatallah, R., Gagnot, G., Pinto, G. P., Santana, V. T., Stuchla, M., Neugebauer, P., Chaiyen, P., Damborsky, J., Bednar, D., Janin, Y. L., Prokop, Z., Marek, M., 2023, *Nature Catalysis* 6: 23-38.

![](https://loschmidt.chemi.muni.cz/peg/wp-content/uploads/2023/01/Nature_Catalysis_graphical_abstract-300x167.jpg)

![](https://loschmidt.chemi.muni.cz/peg/wp-content/uploads/2023/01/Nature_Catalysis_graphical_abstract-300x167.jpg)

Here we decipher Renilla-type catalysis through crystallographic, spectroscopic and computational experiments. Structures of ancestral and extant luciferases complexed with the substrate-like analogue azacoelenterazine or a reaction product were obtained, providing molecular snapshots of coelenterazine-to-coelenteramide oxidation. The results obtained also reveal structural features distinguishing flash-type from glow-type bioluminescence, providing insights that will guide the engineering of luciferases for diverse bioluminescent technologies.

[Read more](https://loschmidt.chemi.muni.cz/peg/publications/catalytic-mechanism-for-renilla-type-luciferases/)

## Advanced Database Mining of Efficient Biocatalysts by Sequence and Structure Bioinformatics and Microfluidics

Vasina, M., Vanacek, P., Hon, J., Kovar, D., Faldynova, H., Kunka, A., Buryska, T., Badenhorst, C.P.S., Mazurenko, S., Bednar, D., Stavrakis, S., Bornscheuer, U.T., deMello, A., Damborsky, J., Prokop, Z., 2022, *Chem Catalysis* 2: 1-22.

![](https://loschmidt.chemi.muni.cz/peg/wp-content/uploads/2022/10/Vasina_Chem_Catal_Graphical_Abstract_2022_2_upr-300x169.png)

![](https://loschmidt.chemi.muni.cz/peg/wp-content/uploads/2022/10/Vasina_Chem_Catal_Graphical_Abstract_2022_2_upr-300x169.png)

We present a pipeline integrating sequence and structural bioinformatics with microfluidic enzymology to discover efficient and robust haloalkane dehalogenases. The bioinformatic part identified 2,905 putative dehalogenases and prioritized a “small-but-smart” set of 45 genes, yielding 40 active enzymes, 24 of which were biochemically characterized by microfluidic enzymology techniques. The obtained biocatalysts outperform the previously known wild-type and engineered dehalogenases. This strategy is applicable to other enzyme families, paving the way towards accelerating the identification of novel biocatalysts for industrial applications.

[Read more](https://loschmidt.chemi.muni.cz/peg/publications/advanced-database-mining-of-efficient-biocatalysts-by-sequence-and-structure-bioinformatics-and-microfluidics/)

## Mechanism-Guided Tunnel Engineering to Increase the Efficiency of a Flavin-Dependent Halogenase

Prakinee, K., Phintha, A., Visitsatthawong, S., Lawan, N., Sucharitakul, J., Kantiwiriyawanitch, C., Damborsky, J., Chitnumsub, P., van Pée, K.-H., Chaiyen, P., 2022, *Nature Catalysis* 5: 534-544.

![](https://loschmidt.chemi.muni.cz/peg/wp-content/uploads/2022/06/TOC_Nature_Catalysis-300x191.png)

![](https://loschmidt.chemi.muni.cz/peg/wp-content/uploads/2022/06/TOC_Nature_Catalysis-300x191.png)

Although flavin-dependent halogenases are attractive for C–H bond activation, their applications are limited due to low turnover and stability. Here we employed a mechanism-guided semi-rational approach to engineer the intermediate transfer tunnel connecting two active sites of tryptophan 6-halogenase Thal. This Thal-V82I variant generates less hypohalous acid leakage and possesses multiple catalytic improvements such as faster halogenation, broader substrate utilization, and greater thermostability and pH tolerance compared with the wild-type Thal.

[Read more](https://loschmidt.chemi.muni.cz/peg/publications/mechanism-guided-tunnel-engineering-to-increase-the-efficiency-of-a-flavin-dependent-halogenase/)

## CalFitter 2.0: Leveraging the Power of Singular Value Decomposition to Analyse Protein Thermostability

Kunka, A., Lacko, D., Stourac, J., Damborsky, J., Prokop, Z., Mazurenko, S., 2022, *Nucleic Acid Research* gkac249: 1-9.

![](https://loschmidt.chemi.muni.cz/peg/wp-content/uploads/2022/05/Kunka_CalFitter2.0_TOC_2022_web-1-300x104.png)The quantitative description of protein unfolding and aggregation is crucial for rational protein design and unravelling the molecular basis of protein misfolding diseases. To assist in the analysis of data from protein denaturation experiments, we have released CalFitter 2.0, which newly incorporates singular value decomposition. Various protein spectra can now be analyzed globally, dramatically reducing the risk of human bias and providing valuable insights into spectral fingerprints of unfolding intermediates. CalFitter 2.0 is the only freely available tool that combines singular value decomposition of unfolding kinetic and temperature scanning experiments with global data analysis. The tool is freely available at <https://loschmidt.chemi.muni.cz/calfitter>.

![](https://loschmidt.chemi.muni.cz/peg/wp-content/uploads/2022/05/Kunka_CalFitter2.0_TOC_2022_web-1-300x104.png)

[Read more](https://loschmidt.chemi.muni.cz/peg/publications/calfitter-2-0-leveraging-the-power-of-singular-value-decomposition-to-analyse-protein-thermostability/)

## Mechanism-Based Strategy for Optimizing HaloTag Protein Labeling

Marques, S. M., Slanska, M., Chmelova, K., Chaloupkova, R., Marek, M., Clark, S., Damborsky, J., Kool, E. T., Bednar, D., Prokop, Z., 2022, *JACS Au* 2: 1324−1337.

![](https://loschmidt.chemi.muni.cz/peg/wp-content/uploads/2022/05/Marques_Mechanism-based_Strategy_JACS_TOC_2022-300x173.jpg)

![](https://loschmidt.chemi.muni.cz/peg/wp-content/uploads/2022/05/Marques_Mechanism-based_Strategy_JACS_TOC_2022-300x173.jpg)

HaloTag labelling technology introduced unrivalled potential in protein chemistry and biology and it quickly became the dominant tagging technology. Surprisingly, despite the huge number of diverse applications and different available ligands, a single tagging enzyme is repeatedly used without considering the selection of other enzyme variants that are more suitable for a specific ligand. Our results show that natural dehalogenases provide high efficiency of the various ligand incorporation with no need for intensive optimization using directed evolution. Based on these findings, we propose a simple strategy for designing efficient HaloTag reactions, which consist of finding the optimal enzyme tag for specific ligand from a wide range of natural dehalogenases using easy-to-use in silico tools.

[Read more](https://loschmidt.chemi.muni.cz/peg/publications/mechanism-based-strategy-for-optimizing-halotag-protein-labeling/)

## LoopGrafter: a Web Tool for Transplanting Dynamical Loops for Protein Engineering

Planas-Iglesias, J., Opaleny, F., Ulbrich, P., Stourac, J., Sanusi, Z., Pinto, G. P., Schenkmaye- rova, A., Byska, J., Damborsky, J., Kozlikova, B., Bednar, D., 2022, *Nucleic Acid Research* gkac249: 1-9.

![](https://loschmidt.chemi.muni.cz/peg/wp-content/uploads/2022/04/Planas-Iglesias_NAR_2022_TOC-300x225.png)The transplantation or grafting of particularly dynamic loop structures from a source protein to a target one has shown to be a powerful approach to improve the properties of enzymes, and yet no tool is available to assist scientists in approaching this task. LoopGrafter is designed to fill in this gap by accepting as input 3D coordinates of two structurally-related source and target proteins and guiding the user in the process of identifying similar loops among the proteins and assessing their dynamics. Upon selection of the loops to be grafted, all possibilities for recombination points are explored, three-dimensional models built, and their goodness assessed by MODELLER and Rosetta Fast Relax. LoopGrafter is available at <https://loschmidt.chemi.muni.cz/loopgrafter/>.

![](https://loschmidt.chemi.muni.cz/peg/wp-content/uploads/2022/04/Planas-Iglesias_NAR_2022_TOC-300x225.png)

[Read more](https://loschmidt.chemi.muni.cz/peg/publications/loopgrafter-a-web-tool-for-transplanting-dynamical-loops-for-protein-engineering/)

## Extended Mechanism of the Plasminogen Activator Staphylokinase Revealed by Global Kinetic Analysis: 1000-fold Higher Catalytic Activity than That of Clinically Used Alteplase

Toul, M., Nikitin, D., Marek, M., Damborsky, J., Prokop, Z., 2022, *ACS Catalysis* 12: 3807-3814.

[![](https://loschmidt.chemi.muni.cz/peg/wp-content/uploads/2022/04/Toul_ACS_Catalysis_TOC.tif)](https://loschmidt.chemi.muni.cz/peg/wp-content/uploads/2022/04/Toul_ACS_Catalysis_TOC.tif)![](https://loschmidt.chemi.muni.cz/peg/wp-content/uploads/2022/04/Toul_ACS_Catalysis_TOC.tif)[![](https://loschmidt.chemi.muni.cz/peg/wp-content/uploads/2022/04/Toul_ACS_Catalysis_TOC.tif)](https://loschmidt.chemi.muni.cz/peg/wp-content/uploads/2022/04/Toul_ACS_Catalysis_TOC.tif)  
[![](https://loschmidt.chemi.muni.cz/peg/wp-content/uploads/2022/04/Toul_ACS_Catalysis_TOC-300x191.png)](https://loschmidt.chemi.muni.cz/peg/wp-content/uploads/2022/04/Toul_ACS_Catalysis_TOC.tif)

![](https://loschmidt.chemi.muni.cz/peg/wp-content/uploads/2022/04/Toul_ACS_Catalysis_TOC.tif)
![](https://loschmidt.chemi.muni.cz/peg/wp-content/uploads/2022/04/Toul_ACS_Catalysis_TOC.tif)
![](https://loschmidt.chemi.muni.cz/peg/wp-content/uploads/2022/04/Toul_ACS_Catalysis_TOC.tif)
![](https://loschmidt.chemi.muni.cz/peg/wp-content/uploads/2022/04/Toul_ACS_Catalysis_TOC-300x191.png)

We probed the complex kinetic mechanism of staphylokinase, an attractive thrombolytic drug candidate that is active in a multiprotein form with complex regulation. Using a modern global numerical approach, we found that the net value of its catalytic efficiency is 10,000-fold higher than previously determined by conventional methods limited by simplifying assumptions and approximations. We uncover the main catalytic bottleneck whose elimination might provide orders of magnitude more active alternative to the clinically used thrombolytic drug alteplase. Our findings pave the way for the development of a new generation of drugs effectively treating cardiovascular diseases.

[Read more](https://loschmidt.chemi.muni.cz/peg/publications/extended-mechanism-of-the-plasminogen-activator-staphylokinase-revealed-by-global-kinetic-analysis-1000-fold-higher-catalytic-activity-than-that-of-clinically-used-alteplase/)

## Computational Enzyme Stabilization Can Affect Folding Energy Landscapes and Lead to Catalytically Enhanced Domain-Swapped Dimers

Markova, K., Kunka, A., Chmelova, K., Havlasek, M., Babkova, P., Marques, S. M., Vasina, M., Planas-Iglesias, J., Chaloupkova, R., Bednar, D., Prokop, Z., Damborsky, J., Marek, M., 2021, *ACS Catalysis* 11: 12864-12885.

![](https://loschmidt.chemi.muni.cz/peg/wp-content/uploads/2021/10/Markova_Computational_Enzyme_Stabilization_Graphical_abstract-300x170.png)

![](https://loschmidt.chemi.muni.cz/peg/wp-content/uploads/2021/10/Markova_Computational_Enzyme_Stabilization_Graphical_abstract-300x170.png)

Computer-encoded algorithms are increasingly employed to stabilize native proteins for research, biotechnologies, and biomedical applications. Methods such as [FireProt](https://loschmidt.chemi.muni.cz/fireprotweb/) are available to the research community as web services, making computer-aided design of multiple mutants extremely easy and widely accessible. This study reports important findings related to potential changes in the folding pathway and catalytic properties of designed hyperstable mutants. Structural analysis revealed that stabilizing mutations may activate cryptic hinge-loop regions and establish new secondary interfaces, leading to the formation of so-called domain-swapped dimer.

[Read more](https://loschmidt.chemi.muni.cz/peg/publications/computational-enzyme-stabilization-can-affect-folding-energy-landscapes-and-lead-to-catalytically-enhanced-domain-swapped-dimers/)

## Engineering the Protein Dynamics of an Ancestral Luciferase

Schenkmayerova, A., Pinto, G. P., Toul, M., Marek, M., Hernychova, L., Planas-Iglesias, J., Daniel Liskova, V., Pluskal, D., Vasina, M., Emond, S., Dörr, M., Chaloupkova, R., Bednar, D., Prokop, Z., Hollfelder, F., Bornscheuer, U. T., Damborsky, J., 2021, *Nature Communications* 12: 3616.

![](https://loschmidt.chemi.muni.cz/peg/wp-content/uploads/2021/06/FeaturedImage_01-300x169.png)

![](https://loschmidt.chemi.muni.cz/peg/wp-content/uploads/2021/06/FeaturedImage_01-300x169.png)

We track the role of dynamics in evolution, starting from the evolvable and thermostable ancestral protein AncHLD-RLuc which catalyses both dehalogenase and luciferase reactions. Insertion-deletion backbone mutagenesis of AncHLD-RLuc challenged the scaffold dynamics. Screening for both activities reveals mutations localized in three distinct regions that lead to altered protein dynamics. Transplantation of a highly dynamic fragment leads to highly stable glow-type bioluminescence. The success of our approach suggests that a strategy comprising (i) constructing a stable and evolvable template, (ii) mapping functional regions by backbone mutagenesis, and (iii) transplantation of dynamic features, can lead to functionally innovative proteins.

[Read more](https://loschmidt.chemi.muni.cz/peg/publications/engineering-the-protein-dynamics-of-an-ancestral-luciferase/)

## Exploring Mechanism of Enzyme Catalysis by On-Chip Transient Kinetics Coupled with Global Data Analysis and Molecular Modeling

Hess, D., Dockalova, V., Kokkonen, P., Bednar, D., Damborsky, J., deMello, A., Prokop, Z., Stavrakis, S., 2021, *Chem* 7: 1-14.

![](https://loschmidt.chemi.muni.cz/peg/wp-content/uploads/2021/03/Exploring_Mechanism_of_Enzyme_Catalysis_Graph_Abstract-1-300x300.jpg)

![](https://loschmidt.chemi.muni.cz/peg/wp-content/uploads/2021/03/Exploring_Mechanism_of_Enzyme_Catalysis_Graph_Abstract-1-300x300.jpg)

We develop a droplet-based microfluidic platform for high-throughput acquisition of transient kinetic data over a range of substrate concentrations and temperatures. To demonstrate their utility, we measure the transient kinetics of three model enzymes, namely, b-galactosidase, horseradish peroxidase, and microperoxidase. Additionally, we conduct a c

# [Colorado State University](https://www.colostate.edu)

# Interdisciplinary team uses AI powered tools for protein engineering in medicine and beyond

By [Josh Rhoten](https://source.colostate.edu/author/c824188513/)

# AI-powered research

###### Interdisciplinary team uses AI-powered tools for protein engineering in medicine and beyond

By Josh Rhoten  
Published April 10, 2025

Interdisciplinary researchers at Colorado State University are using AI powered tools to rapidly re-engineer and discover new proteins – a fundamental building block of all life as we know it. Their work is part of a growing movement to apply artificial intelligence techniques to better understand how living cells operate, with broad applications in medicine, including in the battle against infectious diseases.

Proteins are microscopic molecules that dictate everything from the behavior of viruses and bacteria to the regular operation of the human body. These tiny catalysts are made of a unique string of amino acids that determine both their 3D structure and function. Researchers have worked for decades to predict how proteins fold into these unique shapes to then build muscle or regulate hormones, among many other key processes.

These complex shapes can also inform the ways antibodies spot and react to diseases attacking our immune systems. With that in mind, researchers have been working to find or engineer proteins that can then be used as powerful tools in treatments such as drug development or to improve our basic understanding of cell function.

[Christopher Snow](https://www.engr.colostate.edu/cbe/people/chris-snow/), a professor in the [Department of Chemical and Biological Engineering](https://www.engr.colostate.edu/cbe/), said discovering and then outlining a protein’s shape was a time intensive process in the past. However, AI tools now allow researchers to quickly identify and predict how proteins may interact and specifically link together like a lock and key with other molecules for a given purpose or need.

New AI tools can also help guide researchers to genetically design proteins that may not even exist in nature but would be useful, said Snow.

“I have always tried to embrace the latest technology in my lab, and AI is clearly changing the field of biomolecular engineering,” he said. “The combination of tools available to us presents an exciting opportunity to make the research and identification processes more effective.”

Snow said Google DeepMind’s AlphaFold2 first broke barriers in 2020 and is still the most popular and effective AI tool for protein structure prediction today. Depending on the size of the target, high-confidence models can be made in minutes with the system about a given protein’s structure when it folds. Since then, third generation methods, such as AlphaFold3 and its competitors, have emerged and extend the predictions to non-protein components like small molecules and nucleic acids. In many cases, the predictions are good enough to enable understanding without time-consuming real-life experimentation.

“That capability was a holy grail for the field for decades. Since the introduction of this neural network, we can now rapidly predict shapes with an accuracy that is competitive with what may come out of long, physical experimentation,” Snow said. “We used to have to cut and copy fragments of protein antibodies together, for example, to see if they were compatible and or useful for a given purpose we were pursuing. That worked OK, but machine learning has improved that process dramatically.”

Snow also pointed to two other AI tools his team was leveraging for this work. ProteinMPNN – a neural network developed at the University of Washington – looks at a given protein’s 3D structure and then predicts optimized amino acid sequences for that target structure. Those tweaks could make the proteins more stable or improve their overall function compared to naturally occurring versions.

Snow also uses RFdiffusion to invent totally new protein backbone structures. The system develops these potential leads for researchers to consider in a similar way to AI art generation software. Those AI systems are trained to remove random noise that has been added to existing images and, ultimately, they can then generate altogether new images starting from pure noise. Here the system considers what it knows about the shapes of existing 3D protein structures to see if new workable combinations can be found from the endless possible combination of shapes available. This process generates avenues for researchers to further explore in pursuit of vaccines and other medical treatments.

Snow said his team uses these tools and others in their daily work.

“These AI tools are not perfect, but their makers knew that and included aspects in their code that point out where the system is more confident and less confident in its findings,” he said. “The applications are quite broad and have really accelerated our ability to now design needed biomolecules, biomaterials, and devices.”

## Interdisciplinary biomolecular engineering research at Colorado State

Snow has been working with these AI tools in partnership with professors [Brian Geiss](https://vetmedbiosci.colostate.edu/directory/member/?id=brian-geiss-3203), from the Department of Microbiology, Immunology, and Pathology, and [Tim Stasevich](https://stasevichlab.colostate.edu/), from the Department of Biochemistry and Molecular Biology. All three faculty also hold appointments in the [School of Biomedical Engineering at CSU](https://www.engr.colostate.edu/sbme/). Together, the group is known as TagTeam on campus and includes former Postdoctoral Researcher Ning Zhao, who is now a faculty member at the University of Colorado Anschutz.

While they agree that biomolecular engineering has broad applications even beyond medicine, the team is particularly interested in studying the inner workings of the cell with this technology.

“I have always tried to embrace the latest technology in my lab, and AI is clearly changing the field of biomolecular engineering. The combination of tools available to us presents an exciting opportunity to make the research and identification processes more effective.”

Christopher Snow

Professor, Chemical and Biological Engineering

Scientists currently use a method where they attach a second glowing protein to an existing protein to watch it move inside a cell and study its location and interactions. But that approach might ultimately change how the protein behaves. Instead, the CSU team is working to engineer special proteins that can be tracked as they find and stick to natural proteins inside cells without needing those permanent glowing tags, Geiss said.

“We have traditionally had to add big fluorescent proteins to host or viral proteins in order to see the processes we are interested in, which is like hooking a U-Haul trailer to a race car and expecting the race car to be as fast as normal,” Geiss said. “Being able to specifically engineer antibodies and novel proteins that then recognize natural proteins in living cells is a dream come true for cell biologists and virologists, since it lets us see how they behave in a much more natural setting.”

The focus of this collaborative work and technology has been studying how antibody proteins can recognize other proteins – especially viral proteins – through a part of their 3D structures called epitopes. An epitope is a small part of a protein that enables the larger immune system to recognize the threat as it battles infection.

Antibodies produced by our immune systems can recognize specific epitopes to protect against disease, and they have long been used as molecular magnets to detect proteins in cells. The TagTeam has been using their expertise to now design antibodies that can detect viral proteins in living cells. This allows researchers to view how these key proteins move and interact in cells in real time, providing much richer information about these complicated systems.

AI tools like AlphaFold2 are used to quickly predict where proteins could potentially attach. For instance, the team successfully used the program to predict the binding site of a new COVID-19 antibody. Stasevich said they confirmed those findings with lab experiments, and the project was published in two manuscripts – in eLife in January 2025 and the other still in preprint form.

“The more you know about where and when virus proteins go in a cell and when the virus is replicating, the more information you have to develop interventions and therapeutic applications like vaccines,” Stasevich said. “Our approach lets us see the inner workings of the cell in great resolution and assists in understanding how antibodies bind their targets.”

# Interdisciplinary Protein Design

![](/project/interdisciplinary-protein-design/featured_hu4ba409ab8675862d7124ff5056dfd66a_631573_720x0_resize_q75_lanczos.jpg)

Our lab is interdisciplinary. One part of the research entails conducting *in silico* protein design, and the other part is wet-leb experimental screening of these designs. If you read through both the [computational](/project/computational-design/) and the [experimental](/project/high-throughput-engineering/) project descriptions and you canât decide, because both sounds great - congratulations! You can join our lab and learn / apply both aspects and conduct truely interdisciplinary research. That is the same way as Max has learned the trade, and although you might not dive as deep into certain techniques as a more specialised researcher, your flexibility and broad knowledge will compensate you double. Interdisciplinary training is also the best preparation for a career outside academia, where broad knowledge and skills are highly valued.

**Join Us**

The University of Michigan, Ann Arbor is one of the great research institutions of the world. We are actively looking for passionate, highly motivated scientists at all levels to join our interdisciplinary team at U of M. Scientists interested in microbial biochemistry, structural biology, cryo-EM, protein organelles, natural products, enzymology, protein engineering, or synthetic biology are encouraged to apply! Prior training in biochemistry, chemistry, biological or chemical engineering or structural biology is highly encouraged and preferred.

If interested, please e-mail Tobias at tgiessen@umich.edu with your CV (including a list of references), funding suggestions, summary of your prior research experience and future research interests.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Interdisciplinary teams combine diverse expertise, fostering innovation in protein engineering. This collaboration enhances problem-solving capabilities and accelerates advancements by integrating knowledge from various scientific fields.

Partnerships between academic institutions and industry drive practical applications of research. These collaborations enable the translation of scientific discoveries into real-world technologies, enhancing protein engineering's impact on society.

International collaborations and initiatives promote knowledge sharing and resource pooling. These efforts aim to address global challenges in health, agriculture, and sustainability through advanced protein engineering solutions.
